figures.gloss: The Sky Father locked in embrace with Papatūānuku the Earth Mother until their children pried them apart.
figures.facet: Here the sky-father is a lover and a prisoner, not a king — and prying him loose is the birth of the world.

figures.gloss: The Earth Mother, held apart from her sky-husband Ranginui so that light and life can fill the space between.

Son of Ranginui and Papatūānuku, atua of the forests, birds and growing things. In the council of the cramped children it is Tāne who, lying on his back and thrusting with his legs, at last forces the Sky-Father up off the Earth-Mother so that light may flood between them. He props the sky on poles, clothes his father in stars, and later fashions Hine-ahu-one, the first woman, from the red earth of Kurawaka.
figures.facet: The shard where the world is born from a SON'S strength against his own parents: separation is not a god's decree but a child's straining limbs, and the same hands that pry heaven loose then mould the first human from soil — maker of the lit space and of the people who will stand in it.
figures.provenance: Grey, 'Polynesian Mythology'; Best, 'Maori Religion and Mythology'; Tregear, 'Maori-Polynesian Comparative Dictionary'. The separation-and-creation of Hine-ahu-one is the central Māori cosmogonic act.

figures.gloss: Fiercest of the brothers, atua of war and of humankind. Alone among the children he stands his ground against the storm-brother Tāwhirimātea and is not driven off. In revenge upon the siblings who abandoned him to the gale, Tū hunts and eats the children of his brothers — Tāne's birds, Tangaroa's fish, Rongo's and Haumia's plants — and by this devouring establishes humanity's right to take all of them as food.
figures.facet: Humanity's ancestor is the brother who would NOT flinch and who answered desertion by eating his kin's offspring: the karakia of the hunt, the net and the digging-stick all descend from Tū's act, so the right to consume the world is founded in an originary fratricidal appetite, not a gift.
figures.provenance: Grey, 'Polynesian Mythology'; Best, 'Maori Religion and Mythology'. Tū is invoked as the ancestor-atua of warriors and in rites of war and first-fruits.

figures.gloss: Son of Rangi and Papa, atua of wind, cloud and tempest. He ALONE among the brothers opposed the parting of their parents, and when it was done he followed his father Ranginui up into the sky in fury. From there he loosed his winds, rains and squalls upon his brothers — snapping Tāne's forests, churning Tangaroa's sea, hounding all but unbending Tū — and rages against them still.
figures.facet: The storm here is GRIEF and dissent made weather: Tāwhirimātea is the one child loyal to the embrace, who could not bear to see his parents torn apart, and every gale is his unhealed protest at the separation that made the world — the cosmic order itself founded over a brother's refusal.
figures.provenance: Grey, 'Polynesian Mythology'; Orbell, 'Concise Encyclopedia of Māori Myth and Legend'. His standing war on his siblings is the Māori etiology of storms.

figures.gloss: Son of Rangi and Papa, atua of cultivated foods — above all the kūmara (sweet potato) — and of peace and the arts of the planted field. With his brother Haumia he hides in the body of their mother Papatūānuku when the storm assails the children, and from her flesh the food-plants of the garden are drawn. His name means 'sound' or 'tidings', and he presides over the rituals of planting and harvest.
figures.facet: The cultivated harvest is a brother who SHELTERS in the earth-mother's body and is plucked from her flesh — agriculture as a child taking refuge in his mother, so the kūmara crop is literally of Papatūānuku, and Rongo holds peace as the counter-pole to war-faced Tū.
figures.provenance: Grey, 'Polynesian Mythology'; Best, 'Maori Agriculture'. Rongo is cognate with wider Polynesian Lono/Ro‘o, an attested Polynesian descent though glossed only here.

figures.gloss: Son of Rangi and Papa, atua of the uncultivated foods that grow wild — chiefly the aruhe, the rhizome of the bracken fern that was a staple of the old Māori diet. Like his brother Rongo he hides within their mother Papatūānuku from Tāwhirimātea's storm, his fern-root buried in her body, and is dragged forth to be eaten by Tū's descendants.
figures.facet: The wild harvest set as the twin of the sown: where Rongo is the planted garden, Haumia is the food that needs no tending — so even the fernroot dug from the hillside is a divine brother sheltering in the earth-mother, and Māori subsistence is split between cultivated and foraged at the root of creation.
figures.provenance: Grey, 'Polynesian Mythology'; Best, 'Maori Religion and Mythology'. Haumia and Rongo are consistently paired as the wild and cultivated foods hidden in Papatūānuku.

figures.gloss: Goddess of death and ruler of the Pō, the night-world to which the dead return. She is Hine-ahu-one's daughter Hine-tītama, who on learning that her husband Tāne was also her father fled in shame to the underworld to receive her descendants as the dead. When Māui sought to win immortality by crawling through her sleeping body, she woke and crushed him in the obsidian teeth between her thighs — so that death has never since been undone.
figures.facet: Death is a SHE who was wronged and went down willingly: the first woman's daughter, fleeing the discovery of incest, becomes the gentle gatherer of all her offspring in the dark — and the one barrier no trickster passes, the threshold where even Māui's cunning is ground to nothing between her thighs.
figures.provenance: Grey, 'Polynesian Mythology' (the death of Māui); Best; Orbell. The Hine-tītama-to-Hine-nui-te-pō transformation is the Māori myth of the entry of death.

figures.gloss: The first woman, fashioned by Tāne from the red earth of Kurawaka, the pubic mound of the Earth-Mother Papatūānuku, and quickened with his breath. Tāne takes her to wife, and their daughter Hine-tītama — who will become Hine-nui-te-pō — is the first human born of woman. Through Hine-ahu-one the line of humankind begins from moulded soil.
figures.facet: The mother of the people is SHAPED, not born — clay of the Earth-Mother's own body breathed into life by the forest-god — so humankind is at once made of Papatūānuku's flesh and fathered by Tāne, the first woman a sculpture before she is an ancestor.
figures.provenance: Grey, 'Polynesian Mythology'; Best, 'Maori Religion and Mythology'. The forming of Hine-ahu-one at Kurawaka is the Māori anthropogony.

figures.gloss: Atua of darkness, disease and death, and Tāne's implacable underworld rival. The two brothers contend over the ascent to the highest heaven to fetch the three baskets of knowledge; Tāne prevails and Whiro, defeated, descends into the underworld (Rarohenga / the Muriwai-hou) whence he sends sickness, evil and decay up into the world of light to gnaw at humankind. He commands the maggots and biting insects, the agents of corruption.
figures.facet: The principled enemy of order as ENVY of the light-bringer's victory: Whiro is the brother who lost the race to heaven and turned the whole apparatus of disease and decay into a standing campaign of revenge — evil here is not chaos but a defeated sibling's grudge, eating the world from below.
figures.provenance: Best, 'Maori Religion and Mythology' (the contest of Tāne and Whiro for the baskets of knowledge); Tregear. Whiro's cosmic-adversary role is most developed in the esoteric whare-wānanga teachings recorded by Best, which are themselves a learned tradition.

figures.gloss: The Māori cosmogony as a graded becoming: from Te Kore, the void of unrealised potential, through the long succession of Te Pō, the many deepening nights (Te Pō-nui, Te Pō-roa), the world ripens until Rangi and Papa appear, are parted by their children, and Te Ao Mārama — the World of Light — breaks forth. Many chants count the Pō in numbered generations, a whakapapa of nothingness slowly quickening into being.
figures.facet: Creation as a GENEALOGY rather than an act: not a maker shaping matter but existence itself reckoned in descent — void begetting night begetting night begetting the parents whose parting is light — so the cosmos is a whakapapa, and to know the world is to be able to recite the long nights from which it grew.
figures.provenance: Best, 'Maori Religion and Mythology'; Tregear; the cosmogonic chants of the whare-wānanga (e.g. as preserved in the teachings of Te Matorohanga). The numbered-Pō genealogies vary between tribal traditions — this is a learned, recited cosmology, not a single fixed text.

figures.gloss: The genealogical method as ontology: all things — atua, people, fish, forests, even the numbered nights of the cosmogony — stand on one recited lattice of descent. To whakapapa is to lay layer upon layer; a person establishes identity, land-right, and relation to any stranger by reciting the lines that join them, and the trained memory that holds those lines was the core discipline of the whare wānanga.
figures.facet: The shard where genealogy is not a record OF the world but the structure of it: knowledge itself is organized as descent, so to know a thing is to be able to place it in the family of being.
figures.provenance: Te Rangi Hīroa (Peter Buck), 'The Coming of the Maori' (1949); Hirini Moko Mead, 'Tikanga Māori' (2003); the cosmogonic whakapapa recorded in Grey's 'Polynesian Mythology' and Best's whare-wānanga material.

figures.gloss: The three-term physics of the Māori sacred: mana, the efficacious ancestral power that persons, lands, and works accumulate or forfeit; tapu, the state of being set under the influence of the atua — restricted, dangerous, demanding care (the chief's person, the burial ground, the new house, the woman in childbirth, each in differing degree); and noa, the ordinary, freed state to which things are deliberately returned by whakanoa rites of water and cooked food.
figures.facet: The complete circuit that kin shards show only in halves: not just power (mana) and prohibition (tapu) but the engineered return-to-ordinary (noa) — the sacred managed as a reversible state, with cooked food and water as its discharge-tools.
figures.provenance: Elsdon Best, 'Spiritual and Mental Concepts of the Maori' (1922); Te Rangi Hīroa, 'The Coming of the Maori' (1949); Mead, 'Tikanga Māori' (2003).

figures.gloss: The supreme being of the esoteric whare-wānanga teachings: Io the parentless, self-existent in the highest of the twelve heavens, from whom Tāne fetched the three baskets of knowledge; his name held too tapu for common speech, his lore — if lore it anciently was — confined to the highest grade of tohunga. Recorded chiefly in S. Percy Smith's 'The Lore of the Whare-wānanga' (1913), from the 1860s teachings of Te Mātorohanga as written down by H. T. Whatahoro.
figures.facet: A genuinely contested figure, carried here as contested: one scholarly reading holds Io a pre-contact esoteric high god hidden from commoners and early missionaries alike; the other holds the tradition a post-missionary construction shaped by Christian monotheism — Te Rangi Hīroa doubted its antiquity and currency, Jonathan Z. Smith argued the case for invention, while Māori theologians such as Māori Marsden affirm the Io tradition from within. The mirror records the debate, not a verdict.
figures.provenance: S. Percy Smith, 'The Lore of the Whare-wānanga' (1913–15); Best, 'Maori Religion and Mythology'; contra: Te Rangi Hīroa, 'The Coming of the Maori' (1949) and Jonathan Z. Smith, 'The Unknown God: Myth in History', in 'Imagining Religion' (1982); affirmed from within by Māori Marsden, 'The Woven Universe' (2003).

figures.gloss: The marae ātea — the open ceremonial ground — and its carved meeting house, whose ridgepole is an ancestor's spine, rafters his ribs, gable-face his head: the community assembles inside the body of its forebear. On this ground the tangihanga unfolds, the days-long funeral in which the dead lie in state, are wept over and addressed directly in speech and song, and are finally farewelled on the spirit-road north to Te Rerenga Wairua at Cape Rēinga, where the wairua descends by the roots of the ancient pōhutukawa and turns toward Hawaiki.
figures.facet: Death handled as prolonged, structured conversation with the still-present dead — the body is spoken to, argued over, never left alone — staged inside a building that is itself an ancestor's body; among the most intact living funerary complexes in the mirror.
figures.provenance: Anne Salmond, 'Hui: A Study of Maori Ceremonial Gatherings' (1975); Mead, 'Tikanga Māori' (2003); Best, 'Spiritual and Mental Concepts of the Maori' (1922) on Te Rerenga Wairua.

figures.gloss: The ancestral homeland over the horizon — Hawaiki-nui, Hawaiki-roa, Hawaiki-pāmamao — from which the founding canoes came, from which the kūmara was fetched, from which the child's soul arrives, and to which the dead return. The name is a true cognate of Savaiʻi (Sāmoa), Havaiʻi (the old name of Raʻiātea), and Hawaiʻi itself — reflexes of one inherited Polynesian homeland-name, *Sawaiki, carried across the ocean.
figures.facet: The axis of this cosmos is DISPLACED: the sacred center is not where the people stand but where they came from — origin, seed-source, and afterlife all sited over the sea-horizon, so the whole religion faces backward along the wake of the canoes.
figures.provenance: Margaret Orbell, 'Hawaiki: A New Approach to Māori Tradition' (1985); Te Rangi Hīroa (1949); the Savaiʻi/Havaiʻi/Hawaiʻi cognate set is standard Polynesian comparative linguistics (POLLEX).

edges.note: The Earth as primal mother, pressed beneath the Sky until the two are forced apart.
Sky-Father and Earth-Mother in unbroken embrace, until their children part them to make the lit world.

Two Oceanic/Australasian sky-creator-ancestors who shape the world and are bound to the heavens — the eaglehawk Bunjil and the sky-father Ranginui — neighbouring oceans, unrelated traditions, both putting the maker in the sky. Structural kin, never a cognate.
Sky-father over earth-mother in both, but Ranginui is separated by force while Nyame recedes of his own holiness — two routes to the same remote sky.
Earth-mother beneath a withdrawn/separated sky-father, keeper of crops and of the dead — the world-parents pattern across unrelated families.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are foundational Earth Mothers from whose literal body or domain humanity emerges as a living crop.
Both place an undifferentiated primal whole at the origin from which the ordered world emerges; world-egg beside the unseparated sky-earth, offered cautiously.
Primal mother-figures of a water-and-earth genesis; Ilmatar floats on the deep to shape the land as Papatūānuku is the earth-mother — independent maternal cosmogonies, no contact.
Sky-and-earth / sun-and-moon parenthood: world-parents whose union and roles order the made cosmos.

Both make a world-body of the cosmos itself — the land borne on the Turtle's living back, the sky a parent-being overhead in Polynesia; a faint world-as-creature echo only, the figures otherwise unalike, flagged.

Kāne IS Tāne by regular sound-law: Proto-Polynesian *Taane > Hawaiian Kāne under the systematic shift *t > Hawaiian k. The same inherited creator-and-light god, name and role retained, split across the Polynesian languages.

Kū continues Proto-Polynesian *Tuu (*t > Hawaiian k); Māori Tū(-matauenga) is the same inherited god of war and the upright — the Hawaiian war-god and the Māori war-god are reflexes of one Polynesian etymon.

Lono < Proto-Polynesian *Rongo by regular reflex — *r > l, *ŋ > n — the cognate of Māori Rongo, god of cultivated food and peace; the same agriculture-and-peace deity descended in two daughter languages.

Both the light, sky-associated, life-giving pole of the Polynesian creative pair — Kāne of the day and fresh water beside the Māori Sky-Father Ranginui; near-kin theologies of the bright above, though Hawaiian thought makes Kāne a maker more than a parted world-parent.

A faint structural rhyme only: both are the dark, lower, receiving pole of a Polynesian dyad — Kanaloa's deep sea against Papatūānuku the Earth-Mother below — but Kanaloa is sea not earth and male not mother; the depth-and-darkness pairing shared, the figures otherwise unalike, flagged.
Both are mother-bodies from which sustenance is drawn, but Sedna is sea and resentment where Papatuanuku is earth and grief — a speculative pairing of provider-mothers wounded into their role.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The primordial Earth Mother forever paired in a sacred dyad with the encompassing Sky Father.

Where the Sky-and-Earth world-parents must be forced apart to make the lived world, the Diné First Man and First Woman are a paired human origin reached by climbing upward through stacked worlds — emergence inverting separation as the shape of beginning.
Both personify a vast elemental domain as the source of sustenance, though one is the sea-chief and one the earth-mother; a loose mother-of-waters/great-source rhyme.

Son against father: it is Tāne who lies on his back and thrusts Ranginui up off the Earth-Mother, propping the sky on poles — the separation that makes the lit world is the work of this child's straining legs.

Tāne pries Papatūānuku free of her sky-husband and then moulds the first woman, Hine-ahu-one, from the red earth of Kurawaka on her body — he frees the Earth-Mother and shapes humanity from her flesh.
The one loyal son: Tāwhirimātea alone opposed the parting and followed his father up into the sky, from where he looses the storms in unending protest at the separation.

Brother against brother and the hinge of creation: Tāne forces the parents apart, Tāwhirimātea rages against it and snaps Tāne's forests in revenge — the maker of the lit world and the storm that mourns it.

The storm drives Tangaroa into the deep; in the flight his sea-children and land-children split, setting ocean against shore — the Māori etiology of why the lizard fled inland and the fish stayed in the sea.

Tū alone stands unbroken before the storm-brother's full assault; the gale cannot drive him off, and his endurance against Tāwhirimātea is why war-faced Tū becomes the ancestor of unyielding humankind.
Revenge on the brothers who fled: Tū hunts and eats Tāne's birds, Tangaroa's fish, and Rongo's and Haumia's plants, founding by that devouring humankind's right to take them all as food.

The cultivated and the wild harvest as twin brothers who both hide in Papatūānuku's body from the storm — Rongo the planted kūmara, Haumia the gathered fernroot, drawn together from the Earth-Mother's flesh.
Peace against war: Rongo-mā-tāne, atua of the planted field and of peace, stands as the counter-pole to his brother Tū the war-faced — the garden and the battlefield as opposed brothers.
Maker and made: Tāne forms Hine-ahu-one from the earth of Kurawaka and breathes her alive, then takes her to wife — the first woman shaped by the same god who pried heaven from earth.

Mother and daughter, life into death: Hine-ahu-one's daughter Hine-tītama flees in shame on learning Tāne is both husband and father, descends to the Pō, and becomes Hine-nui-te-pō, gatherer of the dead.
The two powers of the underworld: Hine-nui-te-pō receives the dead in the Pō, while Whiro sends sickness, decay and the gnawing insects up from below — the gathering-dark and the corrupting-dark.

Light against decay: Tāne and Whiro contend for the ascent to heaven to fetch the three baskets of knowledge; Tāne wins and Whiro, defeated, descends to send disease and evil up against the world of light forever.
The cosmogony delivers the world-parents: out of Te Kore and the long nights of Te Pō, Rangi and Papa at last appear, and their children's parting opens Te Ao Mārama, the World of Light.

Why death stands: Māui crawls into the sleeping Hine-nui-te-pō to win immortality for humankind, but she wakes and crushes him in the obsidian teeth between her thighs — the trickster's one defeat, and the reason death has never been undone.

Both turn on the violent parting of a Sky-and-Earth couple by their children — but the Māori myth puts the deed in a SON'S thrusting legs (Tāne) where the Greek hands it to Kronos's sickle; the world-parents joint reached by two unrelated routes.

Cosmos as an irreducible Heaven-and-Earth polarity emerging from a prior undifferentiation; Qián/Kūn name the primal pair as Te Kore and Te Pō ripen toward Rangi and Papa — a structural rhyme of differentiation, no contact.

Two queens of the night-world who receive all the dead into the dark; Hine-nui-te-pō diverges as the wronged first-daughter who went down by her own shame, the gentle gatherer rather than the dread Mesopotamian sovereign of the great below.

Both atua/god of war and the founder of human martial order; Tū diverges by being also the literal ancestor of humankind, whose originary act is devouring his brothers' children to license the hunt — war and humanity in one figure.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The creation of humanity from earth by Makemake directly adopts the pan-Polynesian myth of Tane.
GOLD SEAM: The primordial earth-mother forcefully coupled with the sky-father in the structural act of cosmic generation.
Sky-being 'who came first' over the lived world; both name the heavens as the prior, originating term of creation.

The recited cosmogony IS a whakapapa: Te Kore begets the numbered nights begets Rangi and Papa — the organizing concept of this shard and the Māori-recorded genealogy-of-nothingness are one instrument.

The atua are genealogy: iwi lines are recited back through Tāne, Tangaroa, or Tū to Rangi and Papa themselves — the world-parents stand at the head of human whakapapa, gods and descendants on one unbroken lattice.

The Māui cycle is written into descent and landscape alike: iwi traditions recite lines from Māui himself, and the North Island is Te Ika-a-Māui, the fish he hauled up, the South Island his canoe — the trickster fixed as ancestor and geography.

GOLD SEAM: the genealogical cosmogony as a shared East Polynesian inherited FORM — the Kumulipo is literally a whakapapa of the cosmos; the chants are independent compositions, but the genre of world-as-recited-descent is common ancestry.

Proven descent: Māori tapu and Hawaiian kapu are regular reflexes of Proto-Polynesian *tapu (*t > Hawaiian k), and mana is the same inherited word in both — one sacred-power-and-prohibition system split across the daughter languages.

Māori tapu and Melanesian tabu/tambu descend from the same Proto-Oceanic *tabu — the etymon Cook carried into English (via Tongan) as 'taboo'; one Oceanic word-and-institution surfacing in two branches of the family.

The mana the moai concentrate and the tapu that shrouded them are the same inherited Polynesian sacred-power vocabulary carried to the mirror's farthest eastern shard — the words are tier-1 cognates; the concept-to-monument deployment is Rapa Nui's own.

In the whare-wānanga teaching it is from Io's highest heaven that Tāne fetches the three baskets of knowledge — but the Io frame around the basket-quest is exactly the stratum whose pre-contact antiquity scholarship contests; the seam is real in the recorded lore, tier-flagged for the debate.

The tangihanga delivers the dead to her: the farewelled wairua goes down into the Pō where Hine-nui-te-pō, the wronged first-daughter, gathers her descendants — the living rite and the death-myth are two ends of one road.
The spirit-road runs from the marae to the homeland: the tangihanga farewells the wairua up the island to Te Rerenga Wairua, the leaping-place of the return to Hawaiki.
Mana descends by whakapapa: a person's sacred power is in large part their recited nearness to the atua and the founding ancestors — the power-system and the genealogy-system are one ranking.

GOLD SEAM: both islands preserve the East Polynesian founding-voyage charter — a named ancestral homeland (Hawaiki / Hiva), chiefly double-hulled canoes, crops carried aboard — the same inherited settlement-tradition genre, independently localized at the two far corners of the triangle.
Two namings of where the dead return: the Hawaiian dead go back into Pō, the generative Night, as the Māori dead voyage back to Hawaiki — the source-realm doubling as destination in both theologies.

GOLD SEAM (substrate): the Māngai required followers to renounce tohunga practice and the old tapu — a Māori prophet dismantling the tapu system from within, even as his movement fought for Māori land and mana.

GOLD SEAM (substrate): Ringatū worship lives on the marae and in carved meeting houses Te Kooti himself raised — Binney documents his house-building, notably Te Tokanganui-a-noho at Te Kūiti (1873) — scripture recited inside the ancestor's body.

dossier.essence: The Polynesian religion of Aotearoa New Zealand, carried by East Polynesian voyagers who settled the islands around 1250–1300 and elaborated it into the classic Māori system encountered by Europeans from 1769. It holds a world of atua (departmental gods and ancestral powers) descended from the world-parents Rangi and Papa, ordered by mana (efficacious ancestral power) and tapu (sacred restriction) — and although mass Christian affiliation came in the 1830s–50s, the marae complex, tangihanga, tapu, and whakapapa remain living practice among Māori today.
dossier.cosmos: The cosmos is a recited genealogy: out of Te Kore (the void of pure potential) descend the numbered nights of Te Pō, then Ranginui the Sky and Papatūānuku the Earth, locked in embrace until their sons — Tāne of the forests foremost — force them apart to let light into the world; Tangaroa takes the sea, Tū war, Rongo the cultivated crop, and human lines are recited back to these same atua on one unbroken lattice. At death the wairua (spirit) travels north up the island to Te Rerenga Wairua at Cape Rēinga, descends by the roots of the ancient pōhutukawa, and turns back toward Hawaiki, the over-the-horizon homeland from which the founding canoes came — origin and destination are the same place.
dossier.practice: Trained tohunga (ritual experts) mediated the atua and taught the tribal lore in the whare wānanga, the school of learning whose core discipline was exact memorized recital of whakapapa and karakia (chant-invocations). Daily life was governed by tapu in differing degrees — the chief's person, the burial ground, the new house, the woman in childbirth — and deliberately discharged back to the ordinary state of noa by whakanoa rites of water and cooked food. Community life centres on the marae and its carved meeting house, built as an ancestor's body, where the tangihanga — the days-long funeral in which the dead lie in state and are wept over and addressed directly — remains the most intact rite of the complex.
dossier.canon: Oral: the cosmogonic and tribal whakapapa, karakia, and waiata held in trained memory, set down in the nineteenth century in works such as Grey's 'Polynesian Mythology' (1855), the Dominion Museum monographs of Elsdon Best, and the whare-wānanga manuscripts of Te Mātorohanga published by S. Percy Smith (1913).
dossier.reflects: Genealogy as ontology — the impulse to make descent the very structure of reality, so that to know anything (god, person, fish, or night of creation) is to be able to recite its place in one family of being.
sources: Elsdon Best, 'Spiritual and Mental Concepts of the Maori' (1922)
Te Rangi Hīroa (Peter Buck), 'The Coming of the Maori' (1949)
Anne Salmond, 'Hui: A Study of Maori Ceremonial Gatherings' (1975)
Hirini Moko Mead, 'Tikanga Māori: Living by Māori Values' (2003)

links.cite: Elsdon Best, 'Spiritual and Mental Concepts of the Maori' (1922)
Te Rangi Hīroa (Peter Buck), 'The Coming of the Maori' (1949)
Anne Salmond, 'Hui: A Study of Maori Ceremonial Gatherings' (1975)
